chunks.chunk_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600_sec428_0_71a74dba
chunks.code_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600
chunks.content:
CHAPTER 38—HIGHER EDUCATION Laboratories 
General Comments 
 
 
  
SBC 801-CC-2024 
1421 
 
—HIGHER EDUCATION LABORATORIES 
GENERAL COMMENTS

❖ Higher education academic institutions often 
have both basic as well as highly advanced 
chemistry, biology, medical, engineering and 
other laboratories where hazardous materials 
are utilized in a nonproduction environment. 
Prior to 2018, this code did not specifically 
address teaching and research laboratories, 
and prior to 1993, model building codes did not 
contain requirements related to “control areas.” 
Users had to comply with the hazardous 
materials provisions, including quantity limits, 
which often could be difficult in these 
specialized academic laboratory settings. It is 
also difficult to apply the control area limits in 
buildings that were not built to those 
requirements, and where control areas might 
not 
exist. 
Also, 
where 
institutions 
are 
landlocked in densely populated areas, the 
buildings often must go upward. However, the 
maximum allowable quantities (MAQs) of 
hazardous materials per control area, and 
detailed in Chapter 50, are extremely restrictive 
on higher floor levels, making it very difficult for 
laboratories to conduct research in taller 
buildings. Further, constructing Group H 
occupancies on higher floor levels can be very 
challenging as well. 
 
These higher education laboratories are 
unique environments 
from a 
hazardous 
materials regulation standpoint. While there is 
often a need for moderate amounts of 
chemicals, the chemicals are usually in small 
containers, which are distributed across 
laboratories on one floor. As a result, the 
overall density of chemicals is low, which helps 
minimize the size of any potential incident. 
Historical fire data over the last 25 years has 
shown that the vast majority of laboratory fires 
do not typically extend beyond the area, or 
even the room, of origin. 
 
Higher education institutions also typically 
have faculty and staff with extensive chemical 
expertise. These “Special Experts” often 
include fire marshals, industrial hygienists, 
radiation safety officers, biological safety 
officers, 
chemical 
hygiene 
officers 
and 
environmental health and safety officers, all of 
whom help ensure the safety of researchers, 
students and visitors, and protect research and 
the buildings. 
 
A typical multiple-story university science 
building will house laboratories, office space, 
storerooms, classrooms, lecture halls and 
other assemblies. Since the standard control 
area MAQs are restrictive on upper floors, often 
designers are forced to locate classrooms and 
lecture halls on the upper floors so that they 
can utilize the higher MAQs allowed on the 
lower floors. This results in moving large 
numbers of students through hallways, past 
laboratories to get to the upper floors. They will 
also have to exit back down the same routes in 
the event of an emergency. 
 
In 
response, 
numerous 
government 
jurisdictions have adopted fire and building 
code amendments and local agreements 
(grandfathering buildings under the 1997 
Uniform Fire Code, for example) to allow 
different 
standards 
for 
educational 
laboratories, particularly on higher floor levels. 
However, this has resulted in varying codes 
and standards across the country, making it 
challenging for designers, the academic 
industry, and fire and building and fire officials. 
In an effort to develop a standardized model 
code approach to this problem, a broad group 
of stakeholders convened, ultimately producing 
this chapter to address these unique and 
challenging issues while keeping safety of the 
occupants a priority.

❖ Purpose 
 
This chapter provides a design option for 
higher education facilities that need to exceed 
the standard control area limitations found in 
Chapter 50. The chapter allows for moderate 
increases in hazardous materials storage and 
use but imposes additional construction and 
operational controls in exchange for the 
increased allowances. Note that Section 428 of 
SBC 201 also contains the provisions on 
laboratory suites, as these are construction-
related issues. 
 
It is important to note that this chapter does not 
automatically apply to all new or existing higher 
education facilities. Only facilities that want to 
use the increased allowances and can meet 
the additional requirements would need to 
comply with this chapter. Fire and building and 
fire officials would only apply this chapter 
-

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600_sec3801_0_7ff777ad
chunks.code_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600
chunks.content:
CHAPTER 38—HIGHER EDUCATION Laboratories 
General Comments

SBC 801-CC-2024 
1422

where the higher education facility intentionally 
chooses to use this option for storage and use 
of hazardous materials. Essentially, this 
chapter is an alternative approach to the 
general hazardous materials requirements 
focused on a specific type of laboratory.

If a facility does not utilize Chapter 38 for 
constructing or operating a laboratory, then the 
laboratories would need to comply with the 
general building and fire code hazardous 
materials control area provisions (SBC 801 
Chapters 50 through 67, SBC 201 Section 414, 
and so on) or construct and operate the 
laboratory as a Group H Occupancy. 
SECTION 3801 GENERAL 
3801.1 Scope. Higher education laboratories 
complying with the requirements of this chapter 
shall be permitted to exceed the maximum 
allowable quantities of hazardous materials in 
control areas set forth in Chapter 50 without 
requiring classification as a Group H occupancy. 
Except as specified in this chapter, such laboratories 
shall comply with all applicable provisions of this 
code and SBC 201.

❖ This chapter provides a design option for 
higher education facilities that need to exceed 
the standard control area limitations found in 
Chapter 50. The chapter allows for moderate 
increases in hazardous materials storage and 
use but imposes additional construction and 
operational controls in exchange for the 
increased allowances. It is very important to 
note that this chapter does not automatically 
apply to any new or existing higher education 
facilities. Only facilities that want to use the 
increased allowances and can meet the 
additional requirements would need to comply 
with this chapter. Note that the occupancy of 
these facilities is also regulated by SBC 201 
and the provisions that allow increases beyond 
the control area concept using laboratory 
suites for new construction are contained in 
SBC 201 Section 428. 
3801.2 Application. The provisions of this chapter 
shall be applied as exceptions or additions to 
applicable requirements of this code. Unless 
specifically modified by this chapter, the storage, 
use and handling of hazardous materials shall 
comply with the provisions in Chapters 50 through 
67 and SBC 201 for quantities not exceeding the 
maximum allowable quantity.

❖ Higher education facilities still must comply 
with all other requirements of Chapters 50 
through 67 and SBC 201 except as modified by 
this chapter. Essentially, this chapter provides 
more flexibility with amounts of materials; 
however, all other provisions in the hazardous 
materials chapters where the MAQs are not 
exceeded must be addressed, such as 
requirements 
for 
incompatible 
materials, 
signage, release of materials and a variety of 
other operational requirements. 
SECTION 3802 DEFINITIONS 
3802.1 Definitions. The following terms are 
defined in Chapter 2: 
CHEMICAL FUME HOOD. 
GLOVE BOX. 
HIGHER EDUCATION LABORATORY. 
LABORATORY SUITE. 
SPECIAL EXPERT.

❖ Definitions 
of 
terms 
can 
help 
in 
the 
understanding and application of the code 
requirements. This section directs the code 
user to Chapter 2 for the proper application of 
the indicated terms used in this chapter. Terms 
may be defined in Chapter 2 or in another 
Saudi Codes as indicated in Section 201.3, or 
the dictionary meaning may be all that is 
needed (see also commentaries, Sections 201 
through 201.4). 
SECTION 3803 GENERAL SAFETY 
PROVISIONS 
3803.1 Scope. Laboratories and laboratory suites 
applying the requirements of this chapter shall be in 
accordance with the general safety provisions in 
Sections 3803.1.1 through 3803.2.2.

❖ This section applies to all facilities using the 
allowances found in Chapter 38. These general 
requirements apply to any facility using any of 
the sections in Chapter 38, including newly 
constructed laboratory suites in accordance 
with Section 3804 (in new or existing 
buildings), 
existing 
laboratories 
in 
nonsprinklered buildings in accordance with 
Section 
3805 
or 
existing 
sprinklered 
laboratories in accordance with Section 3806.

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600_sec3801_1_9c82ea29
chunks.code_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600
chunks.content:
Commentary 
Figure 
3803.1 
provides 
a 
comparison of MAQ possible under Chapter 38 
versus traditional control areas as required in 
SBC 201 Tables 307.1(1) and 307.1(2) and in 
Section 5003.1. 
3803.1.1 
Chemical 
safety 
reviews. 
Operating and emergency procedures planning and 
documentation shall be provided in accordance with 
Sections 5001.3.3.11 through 5001.3.3.17. Such 
documentation shall be prepared by laboratory 
-

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600_sec5003_0_5f917164
chunks.code_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600
chunks.content:
CHAPTER 38—HIGHER EDUCATION Laboratories 
General Comments

SBC 801-CC-2024 
1423

safety personnel or special experts, and shall be 
made available in the workplace for reference and 
review 
by 
employees. 
Copies 
of 
such 
documentation shall be made available to the Fire 
official for review upon request.

❖ This 
section 
requires 
additional 
safety 
preplanning for all laboratories or laboratory 
suites utilizing this chapter, which is consistent 
with laboratory safeguards found in NFPA 45. 
Enhanced safety requirements found in this 
section include the following chemical safety 
reviews, as identified in Sections 5001.3.3.11 
through 5001.3.3.17: process hazard analysis, 
pre-start-up safety review, operating and 
emergency 
procedures, 
management 
of 
change, accident procedures, consequence 
analysis, and safety audits. The requirement 
that such documentation be submitted to the 
Fire official upon request ensures that the 
facility monitors and analyzes the use of 
hazardous materials in laboratories regulated 
under this chapter. 
3803.1.2 
Chemical handling. Receiving, 
transporting on site, unpacking and dispensing of 
hazardous materials shall be carried out by persons 
trained in proper handling of such materials and 
shall be performed in accordance with Chapters 50 
through 67, as applicable.

❖ Because higher education facilities are often in 
mixed-use 
buildings 
and 
chemical 
transportation routes typically cross through 
other spaces or areas, persons transporting 
the hazardous materials must receive training 
in proper handling. 
3803.1.3 
Warning signage. Warning signs 
shall be provided in accordance with Section 
5003.5.

❖ This section refers the user to the warning 
signage provisions of Section 5003.5. As this 
chapter still essentially requires compliance 
with Section 5003 for quantities not exceeding 
the MAQs, much of Section 5003 will apply to 
these laboratories. This will include other 
provisions such as sources of ignition, 
including the prohibition of smoking. 
3803.1.4 
Maintenance 
of 
equipment, 
machinery and processes. Maintenance of 
equipment, machinery and processes used with 
hazardous materials shall comply with Section 
5003.2.6.

❖ This section refers the user to the maintenance 
provisions found in Section 5003.2.6. Hazards 
are reduced by having functional equipment 
and removing and repairing impaired and 
defective equipment. 
3803.1.5 
Time-sensitive 
materials. 
Containers of materials that have the potential to 
become hazardous during prolonged storage shall 
be dated when first opened, and shall be managed 
in accordance with NFPA 45, Section 8.3.4.4.1.

❖ Because 
certain 
materials 
can 
become 
increasingly 
hazardous 
during 
prolonged 
storage, this section mandates additional 
criteria for these materials and refers the user 
to detailed requirements found in NFPA 45. 
The requirements in NFPA 45 focus on 
identifying which materials are time sensitive, 
the inspection frequency for such material, 
defining what inspection procedure should be 
used and what constitutes passing or failing the 
inspection. 
3803.1.6 
Hazardous 
wastes. 
Storage, 
dispensing, use and handling of hazardous waste 
shall comply with this chapter and Chapters 50 
through 67, as applicable.

❖ There is sometimes confusion as to whether 
hazardous wastes are classified as hazardous 
materials under the fire code. This section 
makes it clear that facilities utilizing this chapter 
must treat hazardous waste as a hazardous 
material and comply with this chapter and 
Chapters 50 through 67 as applicable based on 
the hazard class, including complying with the 
maximum allowable quantities established by 
this chapter and Chapter 50. 
3803.1.7 
Automatic 
fire-extinguishing 
systems. New laboratories in new or existing 
buildings that increase maximum allowable 
quantities of hazardous materials based on the 
requirements in this chapter shall be equipped 
throughout with an approved automatic sprinkler 
system in accordance with Section 903.3.1.1.

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600_sec5003_1_3cc9c894
chunks.code_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600
chunks.content:
❖ In order to use the provisions of Chapter 38 in 
any new laboratory (whether in new or existing 
buildings), the entire building shall be equipped 
with an approved automatic sprinkler system. 
This is a significant increase in protection from 
typical B-occupancy laboratories, which can be 
built under Chapter 50 without sprinklers. 
Chapter 38 does have limited provisions to 
allow for existing laboratories in existing 
buildings to utilize some allowances. The 
chapter does not provide clarification on what 
constitutes “new laboratories” in existing 
buildings, or at what point existing laboratories 
undergoing 
modification 
or 
tenant 
improvement would become “new” and require 
fully sprinklered buildings. The Fire official 
would make the decision for what constitutes a 
“new” laboratory. 
-

CHAPTER 50—HAZARDOUS MATERIALS—GENERAL PROVISIONS

SBC 801-CC-2024 
1455

• DISPENSING. 
• EXCESS FLOW CONTROL. 
• EXHAUSTED ENCLOSURE. 
• EXPLOSION. 
• FLAMMABLE VAPORS OR FUMES. 
• GAS CABINET. 
• GAS ROOM. 
• HANDLING. 
• HAZARDOUS MATERIALS. 
• HEALTH HAZARD. 
• IMMEDIATELY DANGEROUS TO LIFE 
AND HEALTH (IDLH). 
• INCOMPATIBLE MATERIALS. LIQUID. 
• LOWER EXPLOSIVE LIMIT (LEL). 
• LOWER FLAMMABLE LIMIT (LFL). 
• MATERIAL 
SAFETY 
DATA 
SHEET 
(MSDS). 
• MAXIMUM ALLOWABLE QUANTITY 
PER CONTROL AREA. 
• NORMAL 
TEMPERATURE 
AND 
PRESSURE (NTP). 
• OUTDOOR CONTROL AREA. 
• PERMISSIBLE EXPOSURE LIMIT (PEL). 
• PESTICIDE. 
• PHYSICAL HAZARD. 
• PRESSURE VESSEL. 
• SAFETY CAN. 
• SAFETY DATA SHEET (SDS). 
• SECONDARY CONTAINMENT.  
• SEGREGATED. 
• SOLID. 
• STORAGE, HAZARDOUS MATERIALS. 
• SYSTEM. 
• TANK, ATMOSPHERIC. 
• TANK, PORTABLE. 
• TANK, STATIONARY. 
• TANK VEHICLE. 
• UNAUTHORIZED DISCHARGE. 
• USE (MATERIAL). 
• VAPOR PRESSURE.

❖ Definitions 
of 
terms 
can 
help 
in 
the 
understanding and application of the code 
requirements. This section directs the code 
user to Chapter 2 for the proper application of 
the indicated terms used in this chapter. Terms 
may be defined in Chapter 2 or in another SBC 
as indicated in Section 201.3, or the dictionary 
meaning may be all that is needed (see also 
commentaries, Section 201through 201.4). 
SECTION 5003 GENERAL 
REQUIREMENTS 
5003.1 Scope. The storage, use and handling of all 
hazardous materials shall be in accordance with this 
section.

❖ Once a chemical is considered a hazardous 
chemical that is not exempt under the 
exceptions of Section 5001.1, it is expected to 
meet the general requirements listed in 
Section 5003and any of the referenced codes 
or standards. 
5003.1.1 
Maximum allowable quantity 
per control area. The maximum allowable quantity 
per control area shall be as specified in Table 
5003.1.1(1) through Table 5003.1.1(4). 
For retail and wholesale storage and display in 
Group M occupancies and Group S storage, see 
Section 5003.11.

❖ This section references Table 5003.1.1(1) 
through Table 5003.1.1(4) for the maximum 
quantities allowed in a control area (see 
Section 5003.8.2for design and protection 
requirements). If the quantity of chemicals 
used in a building does not exceed the 
maximum allowed per control area and the 
number of control areas does not exceed the 
number and percentage of chemicals allowed 
by Table 5003.8.3.2, those areas are 
considered part of the main use and do not 
have to meet all of the requirements for a 
hazardous occupancy.

❖ See Table 5003.1.1(1).

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600_sec5003_2_f64bb15a
chunks.code_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600
chunks.content:
Table 5003.1.1(1) is subdivided based on 
whether the material is in storage or in use in a 
closed or open system. Definitions of both 
“Closed” and “Open” systems are found in 
Section 202. Within these subdivisions, the 
appropriate MAQ is listed in accordance with 
the physical state (solid, liquid or gas) of the 
material. A column for gas in open systems is 
not indicated because gaseous hazardous 
materials should not be allowed in a system that 
is continuously open to the atmosphere. Note 
that the third column of the table, “Group When 
the 
Maximum 
Allowable 
Quantity 
Is 
Exceeded,” 
is 
informational 
only, 
since 
occupancy group determination is within the 
scope of SBC 201, not the code.

Note also that the MAQ values for gases are 
indicated (at the head of each gas column) as 
being “at NTP,” i.e., at a “normal” temperature 
of 21°C and a pressure of one atmosphere, or 
-

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600_sec3804_0_4019324d
chunks.code_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600
chunks.content:
CHAPTER 38—HIGHER EDUCATION Laboratories 
General Comments

SBC 801-CC-2024 
1424

3803.2 Hazardous materials storage and use. 
Hazardous materials storage, handling and use in 
laboratories and laboratory suites complying with 
Chapter 38 shall be in accordance with this chapter 
and Chapters 50 through 67.

❖ Higher education laboratories still need to 
comply with all other requirements of Chapters 
50 through 67 and SBC 201, except as 
modified by this chapter. The requirements are 
related to those that apply where the MAQs 
have not been exceeded. 
3803.2.1 
Container size. The maximum 
container size for all hazardous materials shall be 
20 L for liquids, 22.5 kg for solids, 2.85 m3 for 
health-hazard gases per Table 5003.1.1(2) and 14 
m3 for all other gases in accordance with Table 
5003.1.1(1). 
Exception: Hazardous waste collection containers, 
for other than Class I flammable liquids and Class 
II combustible liquids, are permitted to exceed 20 L 
where approved.

❖ One of the primary controls in Chapter 38 is to 
limit maximum individual container sizes. This 
helps minimize the maximum hazard from an 
incident involving a container or containers. 
These requirements are based largely on 
NFPA 45 limits. It is important to note that these 
maximum container sizes apply in addition to 
MAQ limits. They are not an exception to 
MAQs. For example, if the MAQ for a highly 
toxic gas is 1 m2, then a 10 m2 cylinder would 
not be allowed—the maximum quantity and 
container size are both limited by the MAQ. 
3803.2.2 
Density. Quantities of Class I 
flammable liquids in storage and use shall not 
exceed 30 L per 10 m2 of floor area. Densities shall 
be reduced by 25 percent on the 4th through 6th 
floors of the building, and by 50 percent above the 
6th floor. Regardless of the density, the maximum 
allowable quantity per control area or laboratory 
suite in accordance with this chapter, shall not be 
exceeded. 
Exception: Designated hazardous waste collection 
areas or rooms within a laboratory suite or control 
area are not limited, but such materials shall not 
exceed the maximum allowable quantity per 
laboratory suite or control area.

❖ One of the primary methods to control fire 
hazards in Chapter 38 is to limit maximum 
quantity of flammable liquids in any one area of 
a 
laboratory. 
This 
is 
accomplished 
by 
restricting the maximum density of flammable 
liquid, which is the same method utilized in 
NFPA 45. These density limitations apply in 
addition to MAQ and maximum container size 
limits. The density limitations do not provide an 
exception to either of those other requirements. 
SECTION 3804 LABORATORY SUITE 
CONSTRUCTION 
3804.1 General. Where laboratory suites are 
provided, they shall be constructed in accordance 
with this chapter and Section 428 of SBC 201.

❖ This section provides the concept of laboratory 
suites that can be used as an alternative to the 
general control area concept. The concept is 
similar to those used for control areas. SBC 
201 Section 428 also contains these detailed 
requirements for laboratory suites, since 
technically SBC 201 addresses building 
occupancy and construction classification.

Laboratory suites provide a much higher level 
of fire and hazardous materials protection than 
the traditional control area design described in 
Chapter 
50. 
Besides 
the 
additional 
requirements found in Section 3803, laboratory 
suites are required to have: 
• Rated fire barriers for compartmentation of 
laboratory suites within buildings. 
• Standby or emergency power for safety-
related equipment and enhanced automatic 
sprinkler protection in laboratories above the 
sixth story or in a story below grade plane. 
• Automatic 
sprinkler 
design 
and 
density 
exceeding that which would be required by 
NFPA 45. 
3804.1.1 
Laboratory suites. The number of 
laboratory suites and percentage of maximum 
allowable quantities of hazardous materials in 
laboratory suites shall be in accordance with Table 
3804.1.1.

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600_sec3804_1_593b0997
chunks.code_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600
chunks.content:
❖ Section 3804.1.1 and Table 3804.1.1 identify 
the basic design criteria and number of 
laboratory suites allowed per floor in a building. 
The table then identifies the percentage of 
MAQ that is allowed in each laboratory suite. 
The concept of this table is nearly identical to 
the control area concept.

A laboratory suite may extend horizontally to 
encompass multiple laboratories, or an entire 
floor. A suite may also extend vertically to 
include more than one floor level. If the floor 
levels include floors with differing MAQ limits, 
the code does not specify which limit applies. 
For example, if a laboratory suite extends 
vertically to include suites on the third and 
fourth floors, the code does not prescribe which 
total percentage of MAQ for the entire suite 
would apply. The fire or building official should 
-

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600_sec508_0_4e66c5e3
chunks.code_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600
chunks.content:
CHAPTER 38—HIGHER EDUCATION Laboratories 
General Comments

SBC 801-CC-2024 
1425

make the final determination as to whether the 
third-floor percentage (100 percent), the fourth-
floor percentage (75 percent) or an average of 
the two (87.5 percent) would be applied. The 
fire or building official may want to consider the 
total percent of laboratory suite on each floor or 
other factors in making this decision. A single 
laboratory suite may encompass an entire 
building, as well.

Note c in the table allows the vertical fire 
barriers between laboratory suites on a floor to 
be 1-hour rated because of the reduced 
quantities of hazardous materials in each 
laboratory suite on the floors above the sixth 
story, and additional safety provisions in 
Chapter 38 that apply to all laboratories 
utilizing this chapter. In addition, chemical 
exhaust ducts routed through the 1-hour-rated 
barriers would be permitted to be installed 
without fire dampers, where installed in 
accordance with Section 3804.1.1.7. SBC 201 
Sections 714.1.1 and 717.5.2, Exception 3, 
and Section 607.5.5 of SBC 501 allow exhaust 
system ducts to penetrate fire-rated barriers 
and fire-rated shafts without a fire damper. 
These provisions carry significant importance 
and allow multiple laboratory suites per floor of 
a building supported by a typical HVAC design. 
The note has no effect on other provisions of 
the code and does not change the structural 
fire-resistance requirements of SBC 201 
Chapter 6 or the continuity requirements of 
SBC 201 Chapter 7. 
3804.1.1.1 
Separation 
from 
other 
nonlaboratory areas. Laboratory suites shall be 
separated from other portions of the building in 
accordance with the most restrictive of the 
following: 
1. Fire barriers and horizontal assemblies as 
required in Table 3804.1.1. Fire barriers shall 
be constructed in accordance with Section 
707 of SBC 201 and horizontal assemblies 
shall be constructed in accordance with 
Section 711 of SBC 201. 
Exception: Where an individual laboratory suite 
occupies more than one story, the fire-resistance 
rating of intermediate floors contained within the 
laboratory suite shall comply with the requirements 
of SBC 201. 
2. Separations as required in Section 508 of 
SBC 201.

❖ This section identifies the required separation 
of laboratory suites from nonlaboratory suites. 
For example, the separation of a Group B 
laboratory suite from a Group A-3 lecture hall 
must use the more restrictive of the two 
identified criteria. Primarily, this is an issue 
where the separation requirements are more 
restrictive in SBC 201 Table 508.4 than what 
this section requires. In many cases, if the 
laboratory suite is separated from other 
portions of the building (nonlaboratory suites), 
then Note c allows a 1-hour separation versus 
2 hours, whereas SBC 201 Table 508.4 would 
require 2 hours in the example of Group B and 
A-3 occupancy.

The portions of a building that are not 
laboratory suites would follow all other building 
and fire code requirements. As related to 
hazardous 
materials 
compliance, 
other 
portions of the building that are not laboratory 
suites would follow standard control area 
requirements or could be a Group H 
occupancy. A building can contain both 
laboratory suites and control areas, but the 
total combined number is limited in Section 
3804.1.1.4 
3804.1.1.2 
Separation 
from 
other 
laboratory suites. Laboratory suites shall be 
separated 
from 
other 
laboratory 
suites 
in 
accordance with Table 3804.1.1.

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600_sec508_1_6367ffd6
chunks.code_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600
chunks.content:
❖ This section identifies the required separation 
of laboratory suites from other laboratory 
suites. This, of course, depends on where the 
laboratory suite is located in the building. From 
the seventh story on, the separation increases 
from 1 hour to 2 hours. Note that, as discussed 
in Section 3804.1.1.1, Table 3804.1.1, Note c 
allows a reduction to 1 hour where it is simply 
a separation of a laboratory suite from other 
portions 
of the building 
not containing 
laboratory suites. 
3804.1.1.3 
Floor assembly fire-resistance. 
The floor assembly supporting laboratory suites and 
the construction supporting the floor of laboratory 
suites shall have a fire-resistance rating of not less 
than 2 hours. 
Exception: The floor assembly of laboratory suites 
and the construction supporting the floor of 
laboratory suites are permitted to be 1-hour fire-
resistance- rated in buildings of Types IIA, IIIA and 
VA construction, provided that the building is three 
or fewer stories.

❖ This section requires 2-hour rated floor 
assemblies, regardless of the rating required 
for separation walls. Where 2-hour floor 
assemblies 
are 
required, 
the 
structure 
supporting those floors must also be 2-hour 
rated. The exception provides the only break 
from the 2-hour requirement. Where a building 
is Type IIA, IIIA or VA construction and is three 
-

CHAPTER 38—HIGHER EDUCATION Laboratories 
General Comments

SBC 801-CC-2024 
1426

or fewer stories, the floor assemblies can be 1-
hour fire-resistance rated and supported by 1-
hour construction. 
3804.1.1.4 
Maximum 
number. 
The 
maximum number of laboratory suites shall be in 
accordance with Table 3804.1.1. Where a building 
contains both laboratory suites and control areas, 
the total number of laboratory suites and control 
areas within a building shall not exceed the 
maximum 
number 
of 
laboratory 
suites 
in 
accordance with Table 3804.1.1.

❖ A building can contain both laboratory suites 
and control areas, but the total combined 
number is limited to the maximum number of 
laboratory suites as identified in Table 
3804.1.1. For example, on the second story of 
a building that contains laboratory suites, it 
would be possible to have four laboratory 
suites and two control areas. 
3804.1.1.5 
Means of egress. Means of egress 
shall be in accordance with Chapter 10.

❖ Chapter 38 does not modify any egress 
requirements. Users should refer to Chapter 10 
for egress requirements based on the 
occupancy classification, occupant load and 
other factors prescribed in the code. 
3804.1.1.6 
Standby or emergency power. 
Higher education laboratory suites shall be 
provided with emergency or standby power in 
accordance with Section 1203.2.14.

❖ Standby or emergency power is required for 
any laboratory suite located more than six 
stories above grade plane or any story below 
grade plane. Section 5004.7 identifies the 
types of equipment that must be supplied with 
standby 
or 
emergency 
power. 
These 
provisions are implemented to address the fact 
that it becomes increasingly difficult to respond 
to fires where access for the fire department  is 
more difficult. Thus, the safety systems need to 
able to function longer in order to avoid 
additional failures. 
3804.1.1.7 
Ventilation. Ventilation shall be in 
accordance with SBC 501 and Chapter 7 of NFPA 
45.

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600_sec508_2_f1f8016f
chunks.code_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600
chunks.content:
❖ Laboratory ventilation must be in accordance 
with Chapter 7 of NFPA 45 and SBC 501. 
NFPA 45 has ventilation criteria specific to 
laboratories, including chemical fume hoods, 
local ventilated enclosures, fume arms, special 
local exhaust devices and other systems for 
exhausting air from laboratory work areas in 
which flammable gases, vapors or particulate 
matter 
are 
released. This 
provides 
an 
additional level of safety in laboratories. SBC 
501 Chapters 4, 5 and 6 contain many of the 
general 
construction 
requirements 
for 
ventilation, exhaust systems and duct systems. 
SBC 501 Chapter 5 addresses requirements 
for hazardous exhaust systems, and Chapter 7 
of NFPA 45 further complements those 
provisions 
by 
providing 
very 
specific 
requirements for the type of system or 
hazardous material used. 
3804.1.1.8 
Liquid-tight floor. Portions of 
laboratory suites where hazardous materials are 
present shall be provided with a liquid-tight floor.

❖ As an additional safety precaution, Chapter 38 
requires a liquid-tight floor in all laboratories or 
laboratory suites utilizing Chapter 38. This 
does not require drainage control or secondary 
containment; it only requires that the floor be 
liquid tight to prevent spills from penetrating the 
floor and to allow for cleanup without 
contaminating underlying materials or floor 
levels. This can be accomplished with epoxy or 
other resinous floor systems, impermeable 
floor materials that are monolithic/seamless or 
have welded seams, and other similar 
systems. There are many options available for 
flooring materials and systems designed 
specifically for laboratories that meet the 
requirements of this section. This section also 
inherently requires that any penetrations are 
sealed to maintain liquid-tight integrity. The 
requirement for liquid-tight floors is typically 
only applicable to situations where the 
maximum allowable quantities of hazardous 
materials have been exceeded. 
3804.1.1.9 
Automatic 
fire-extinguishing 
systems. Buildings containing laboratory suites 
shall be equipped throughout with an approved 
automatic sprinkler system in accordance with 
Section 903.3.1.1.

❖ This is a significant modification from standard 
code 
requirements 
(which 
allow 
for 
construction of nonsprinklered laboratories in B 
or F occupancies). New laboratory suites are 
only allowed in fully sprinklered buildings. If a 
user wants to construct new laboratory suites 
with the additional allowances that Chapter 38 
provides, it must be in a fully sprinklered 
building. 
3804.1.2 
Percentage 
of 
maximum 
allowable quantity in each laboratory suite. The 
percentage of maximum allowable quantities of 
hazardous materials in each laboratory suite shall be 
in accordance with Table 3804.1.1.

❖ This section clarifies that the maximum 
percentage of MAQ allowed in a laboratory 
-

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600_sec3805_0_a237b734
chunks.code_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600
chunks.content:
CHAPTER 38—HIGHER EDUCATION Laboratories 
General Comments

SBC 801-CC-2024 
1427

suite are those shown in Table 3804.1.1. This 
is a similar concept to how control areas are 
managed. The farther above or below grade 
you go, the more restrictive the percentages 
become. This relates to the difficulty of 
addressing fires and other emergencies in tall 
buildings and below grade. 
SECTION 3805 NONSPRINKLERED 
LABORATORIES 
3805.1 Scope. Storage and use of hazardous 
materials in existing laboratories located within 
existing buildings not equipped throughout with an 
automatic sprinkler system in accordance with 
Section 903.3.1.1 is permitted where such use 
complies with Section 3803, Chapters 50 through 
67, as applicable, and Sections 3805.2 through 
3805.4.

❖ This section allows very limited increases in the 
quantities of hazardous materials that can be 
used within existing nonsprinklered buildings, 
provided that laboratory operations comply 
with the safety provisions prescribed in this 
chapter. There are a large number of existing 
nonsprinklered laboratories in use throughout 
the United States, many of which were built 
long before the modern I-Codes, and prior to 
the adoption of the use of control areas. Under 
older model codes, buildings could have up to 
100 percent of MAQ on upper floors; however, 
with the more modern I-Codes, that was 
changed to as little as 5 percent on upper 
floors. This section allows some increases and 
modifications in certain scenarios if the 
additional safety controls are provided. This 
section is not intended to allow for the 
construction of new laboratory buildings that 
are not protected throughout utilizing sprinkler 
systems and the laboratory suite concept. 
3805.2 Nonsprinklered 
laboratories. 
The 
maximum allowable quantities of hazardous 
materials in storage and use in control areas in 
laboratories located in buildings not equipped 
throughout with an automatic sprinkler system in 
accordance with Section 903.3.1.1 shall be in 
accordance 
with 
Table 
5003.1.1(1), 
Table 
5003.1.1(2) and Table 5003.8.3.2, except as 
modified by Sections 3805.2.1 and 3805.2.2.

❖ This section clarifies that the control area 
provisions established in Chapter 50 apply, 
except as modified by this section. It is 
important to note that all of the applicable 
additional safety controls in Section 3803 must 
be complied with in order to use the allowances 
in Section 3805. The primary allowance 
provided by this section is that Section 
3803.1.7 is not applicable, since that section 
applies to new laboratories (whether in new or 
existing buildings). Section 3805 is strictly 
limited to existing laboratories in existing 
buildings that do not have a sprinkler system 
installed throughout. 
3805.2.1 
Restricted 
materials 
storage. 
Where approved by the Fire official, storage of the 
following hazardous materials prohibited by Table 
5003.1.1(1) in buildings not equipped throughout 
with an automatic sprinkler system in accordance 
with Section 903.3.1.1 shall be allowed within a 
laboratory control area at 25 percent of Table 
5003.1.1(1) limits for a building equipped 
throughout with an automatic sprinkler system. 
1. Class 4 oxidizers. 
2. Pyrophorics. 
The percentage of the maximum allowable quantity 
per control area shown in Table 3805.4 shall be 
applied to 25 percent of Table 5003.1.1(1) limits for 
Class 4 oxidizers or pyrophoric materials. 
Additional quantity increases shall be prohibited, 
and such materials shall be stored in accordance 
with all of the following: 
1. Containers shall be completely sealed and 
stored in accordance with the manufacturers’ 
recommendations. 
2. Storage shall be within approved hazardous 
material storage cabinets in accordance with 
Section 5003.8.7, or shall be located in an 
inert atmosphere glove box in accordance 
with NFPA 45, Section 7.11. 
3. The storage cabinet or glove box shall not 
contain 
any 
storage 
of 
incompatible 
materials.

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600_sec3805_1_74b9a174
chunks.code_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600
chunks.content:
❖ Where approved by a Fire official, this section 
allows limited storage of Class 4 oxidizers or 
pyrophorics in nonsprinklered laboratories 
where all of the prescribed conditions are met. 
These hazardous materials have caused many 
university laboratories difficulty regarding code 
compliance. 
The 
amounts 
needed 
for 
experimentation are typically very limited.

The following is an example of this allowance:

Given: 
• Laboratory on the fifth story. 
• MAQ pyrophorics 1.8 kg [Table 5003.1.1(1)] 
• Allowance is 12.5 percent of MAQ. (Table 
5003.8.3.2) 
-

CHAPTER 38—HIGHER EDUCATION Laboratories 
General Comments

SBC 801-CC-2024 
1428

• Allowance of this section is 25 percent of Table 
5003.1.1(1), which is limited to 12.5 percent 
due to location; therefore:

❖ 1.8 kg × 0.125 = 0.225 kg

0.225 kg × 0.25 = 0.056 kg

Since this story may have two control areas, a 
total of 0.112 kg would be permitted with a 
maximum of 0.056 kg in each control area. 
3805.2.2 
Restricted materials use. Where 
approved by the Fire official, use of the following 
hazardous 
materials 
prohibited 
by 
Table 
5003.1.1(1) in buildings not equipped throughout 
with an automatic sprinkler system in accordance 
with Section 903.3.1.1, shall be allowed within a 
laboratory control area at 25 percent of Table 
5003.1.1(1) 
limits 
for 
buildings 
equipped 
throughout with an automatic sprinkler system. 
1. Class 4 oxidizers. 
2. Pyrophorics. 
The percentage of the maximum allowable quantity 
per control area shown in Table 3805.4 shall be 
applied to 25 percent of Table 5003.1.1(1) limits for 
Class 4 oxidizers or pyrophoric materials. 
Additional quantity increases shall be prohibited, 
and such materials shall be stored in accordance 
with all of the following: 
1. Use shall be within an approved chemical 
fume hood listed in accordance with UL 
1805, or in an inert atmosphere glove box in 
accordance with NFPA 45, Section 7.11, or 
other approved equipment designed for the 
specific hazard of the material. 
2. Combustible materials shall be kept not less 
than 600 mm away from the work area, 
except for those items directly related to the 
research. 
3. A portable fire extinguisher appropriate for 
the specific material shall be provided within 
6 m of the use in accordance with Section 
906.

❖ Where approved by a Fire official, this section 
allows limited use of Class 4 oxidizers or 
pyrophorics in nonsprinklered laboratories 
where all of the prescribed conditions are met. 
As discussed in Section 3805.2.1, the amounts 
necessary 
for 
experimentation 
in 
the 
laboratories are very limited. But without these 
two sections, those materials would not be 
permitted in any amount. See the example in 
Section 3805.2.1 regarding the calculation of 
the 25 percent. 
3805.3 Restricted materials automatic fire 
detection. An automatic fire detection system shall 
be installed in all existing laboratories in 
nonsprinklered buildings in accordance with this 
section. Detectors shall be connected to the 
building’s fire alarm control unit where a fire alarm 
system is provided. Detector initiation shall activate 
the occupant notification system in accordance with 
Section 907.5 where connected to the building’s fire 
alarm control unit. Activation of the detection 
system shall sound a local alarm in buildings not 
equipped with a fire alarm notification system.

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600_sec3805_2_993a66ae
chunks.code_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600
chunks.content:
❖ Where storage or use of Class 4 oxidizers or 
pyrophorics are allowed in accordance with 
Section 3805.2, automatic fire detection is 
required in accordance with this section. 
Typically, the code requires a smoke detection 
system where an automatic fire detection 
system is required. Section 907.4.3 addresses 
situations where a smoke alarm cannot be 
used and approved heat detectors are 
permitted. 
3805.3.1 
System 
supervision 
and 
monitoring. Automatic fire detection systems shall 
be electronically supervised and monitored by an 
approved supervising station or, where approved, 
shall initiate an audible and visual signal at a 
constantly attended, on-site location.

❖ Where automatic fire detection is required, it 
shall be supervised, or if approved by the Fire 
official, shall initiate a signal at a constantly 
attended, on-site location. 
3805.4 Percentage 
of 
maximum 
allowable 
quantity per control area. The percentage of 
maximum allowable quantities per control area of 
hazardous materials shall comply with Table 
3805.4.

❖ This section and the corresponding table 
identify the number of control areas and 
percentage of the MAQ per control area 
allowed 
in 
an 
existing 
nonsprinklered 
laboratory that meets all of the requirements of 
this section, and the applicable requirements of 
Section 3803. Note that this table is essentially 
a replacement table specific to laboratories. 
This reflects a minor increase in percentage of 
MAQ for facilities that can comply with all of the 
additional requirements. Laboratory suites are 
not allowed in nonsprinklered buildings; 
therefore, this section references traditional 
control areas. Users should refer to Section 
414.2 
of 
SBC 
201 
for 
construction 
requirements of control areas. See the 
following example for how these increases are 
intended to be applied. See also the same

SECTION 3806 EXISTING 
SPRINKLERED LABORATORIES 
3806.1 Scope. Storage and use of hazardous 
materials in existing laboratories within buildings 
equipped throughout with an automatic sprinkler 
system in accordance with Section 903.3.1.1 shall 
be in accordance with Section 3803 and with 
Chapters 50 through 67, as applicable, except as 
modified by this section.

❖ This section allows very limited increases in 
existing laboratories located within existing 
fully sprinklered buildings. There are a large 
number 
of 
existing 
sprinkler-protected 
laboratories in use throughout the United 
States, many of which were built long before 
the modern I-Codes. Under older uniform 
codes, buildings could have up to 100 percent 
of MAQ on upper floors; however, with the 
more modern I-Codes, that was changed to as 
little as 5 percent on upper floors. The code 
recognizes that many laboratories may not be 
able to meet all of the additional controls and 
separations required for laboratory suites. This 
section provides an option to allow for minor 
increases in MAQ, provided that additional 
safety controls are provided. It is important to 
note that all applicable requirements in Section 
3803 must also be complied with for a facility to 
utilize this section. 
3806.2 Hazardous materials storage and use. 
Storage and use of hazardous materials within 
control areas in new and existing laboratories 
equipped with an automatic sprinkler system shall 
be in accordance with this section and Chapters 50 
through 67, as applicable. 
Exception: Existing laboratories in buildings 
equipped throughout with an automatic sprinkler 
system meeting the requirements for laboratory 
suites are permitted to comply with Section 3804.

❖ This section gives existing fully sprinklered 
laboratory buildings three choices: 
1. They can continue to operate within 
traditional Chapter 50 control area limits. 
2. They can upgrade the facility to comply with 
Section 3806, which allows for minor 
increases in percentage of MAQ in the 
control areas. 
3. They can do more significant improvements 
and construct laboratory suites and take 
advantage of larger MAQ increases provided 
in Section 3804. 
3806.2.1 
Percentage 
of 
maximum 
allowable quantities per control area. The 
percentage of maximum allowable quantities per 
control area of hazardous materials shall be in 
accordance with Table 3806.2.1.

❖ This section and corresponding table identify 
the number of control areas and percentage of 
MAQ allowed in existing laboratories in fully 
sprinklered buildings that comply with all 
applicable requirements of Section 3806 and 
the general requirements of Section 3803. This 
table essentially replaces SBC 201 Table 
414.2.2. See the example of the same 
materials addressed in the commentary to 
Section 3805.4.

Given: 
• Three laboratories (control areas) on the third 
floor (this table allows three control areas 
versus two allowed by Table 414.2.2 of SBC 
201 and Table 3805.4 of this code).

Materials: 
• Class 1 flammable liquid. 
• Flammable gas.

MAQs in accordance with Table 3806.2.1 are 
as follows [assuming no increase taken in 
Table 5003.1(1)]:

Class 1 flammable liquid.

115 L × 0.75 = 86.25 L (with sprinkler increase, 
170 L)

Flammable gas.

chunks.content:
CHAPTER 39—Processing and Extraction Facilities 
 
 
  
SBC 801-CC-2024 
1433 
 
—PROCESSING AND EXTRACTION FACILITIES

❖ General Comments 
 
This chapter was created due to the use of 
materials such as flammable gases and 
flammable liquids to process and extract fats 
and oils from plants. Those undertaking these 
processes are typically unfamiliar with the 
hazardous materials requirements found in 
Chapters 50 through 67. This chapter is aimed 
at providing a framework for compliance 
focused on the specific industry related to 
processing and extraction. The occupancy 
classifications will still be based on the 
maximum allowable quantities (MAQ) within 
SBC 201. This chapter focuses on the 
processes. 
 
Although not referenced by this code, NFPA 
36, Standard for Solvent Extraction Plants, can 
be applied to commercial-scale industrial 
facilities. Small-scale operations are not 
covered by the scope of this standard. These 
operations occur in a wide variety of 
commercial 
buildings, 
including 
mixed-
occupancy buildings. Much of the equipment 
used in small-batch extraction is not listed by a 
nationally recognized testing laboratory.

❖ Purpose 
 
Generally, this chapter was developed to deal 
with processes where those undertaking such 
processes 
may 
be 
unfamiliar 
with 
the 
hazardous material requirements of this code. 
In addition, the methods used to process and 
extract oils may be undertaken with equipment 
not addressed by the hazardous material 
requirements of Chapter 50 and material-
specific chapters. This chapter clarifies the 
minimum code requirements for processing 
and extraction operations within buildings. This 
chapter provides a framework for review by the 
Fire official for these facilities and the 
equipment they contain. 
SECTION 3901 GENERAL 
3901.1 Scope. Plant processing or extraction 
facilities shall comply with this chapter and SBC 
201. The extraction process includes the act of 
extraction of the oils and fats by use of a solvent, 
desolventizing of the raw material, production of 
the miscella, distillation of the solvent from the 
miscella and solvent recovery. The use, storage, 
transfilling and handling of hazardous materials in 
these facilities shall comply with this chapter, other 
applicable provisions of this code and SBC 201.

❖ This section establishes the scope of Chapter 
39 with respect to the use of solvents for the 
processing and extraction of fats and oils from 
plants. The requirements in Chapters 50, 53, 
57, 58 and 61 must also be complied with 
where 
applicable. 
These 
provisions 
are 
intended to provide a roadmap on the use of 
the code to address a process not traditionally 
seen as a hazardous process, but where the 
risks are such that special attention must be 
made to address the risks. 
3901.2 Existing buildings or facilities. Existing 
buildings or facilities used for the processing of 
plants shall comply with this chapter. Existing 
extraction processes where the medium of 
extraction or solvent is changed shall comply with 
this chapter.

❖ This section makes it clear that the provisions 
are applicable to new or existing buildings. The 
requirements are based on whether the plant 
processing is occurring within a building. The 
applicability is not related to when the building 
was built. The second sentence focuses on 
assessing the hazard created by the existing 
extraction process or changes in solvent 
utilized for the extraction process. A change in 
solvent would mean that the extraction process 
must comply with the current requirements. 
This 
is 
consistent 
with 
Section 
102.2 
addressing applicability. 
3901.3 Permits. Permits shall be required as set 
forth in Sections 105.5 and 105.6.

❖ 

❖ The process of issuing permits gives the Fire 
official an opportunity to carefully evaluate and 
regulate 
hazardous 
operations. 
Permit 
applicants should be required to demonstrate 
that their operations comply with the code 
before 
the 
permit 
is 
issued. 
See 
the 
commentary to Section 105.5 for a general 
discussion 
of 
operations 
requiring 
an 
operational permit and Section 105.6 for a 
general discussion of activities requiring a 
construction permit. The permit process also 
notifies the fire department  of the need for 
prefire planning for hazardous properties. 
-

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600_sec3902_0_f2a15250
chunks.code_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600
chunks.content:
CHAPTER 39—Processing and Extraction Facilities

SBC 801-CC-2024 
1434

SECTION 3902 DEFINITIONS 
3902.1 Definitions. The following terms are 
defined in Chapter 2: 
• DESOLVENTIZING. 
• MISCELLA.

❖ Definitions 
of 
terms 
can 
help 
in 
the 
understanding and applications of the code 
requirements. This section directs the code 
user to Chapter 2 for the proper application of 
the indicated terms used in this chapter. Terms 
may be defined in Chapter 2 or in another 
Saudi Codes as indicated in Section 201.3, or 
the dictionary meaning may be all that is 
needed (see also commentaries, Sections 201 
through 201.4). 
SECTION 3903 PROCESSING AND 
EXTRACTION 
3903.1 Construction. Processing shall be located 
in a building complying with SBC 201.

❖ As many processing and extraction facilities 
are “cottage industries” or are part of a new 
start-up industry, guidance is necessary to 
clarify that compliance with the applicable 
requirements of SBC 201 are required. This 
section essentially sends you to SBC 201. The 
occupancy classification will be F1 or H based 
on whether the MAQ have been exceeded. If 
the MAQ are exceeded, it is possible to have a 
Group H-2 or H-3 occupancy classification. 
3903.2 Prohibited 
occupancies. 
Extraction 
processes utilizing flammable gases or flammable 
cryogenic fluids shall not be located in any building 
containing a Group A, E, I or R occupancy.

❖

❖ This section is consistent with others in the 
I-Codes in prohibiting potentially hazardous 
operations from occupancies where occupants 
are at a greater risk due to population density, 
lack of mobility or familiarity with the building or 
other factors. There would be much greater 
potential for loss of life should such operations 
occur 
within 
buildings 
containing 
these 
occupancies. 
Processing 
and 
extraction 
systems that utilize flammable liquids or gases 
for the solvent or involve hazardous processes 
create the same type of safety concern as other 
hazardous materials. Similar limits are found 
throughout the code based on level of risk to 
certain occupancies. For example, Section 
5803.1.1 limits the use of flammable gases in 
Group A, E, I or R occupancies and also offices 
in Group B. 
3903.3 Location. The extraction equipment and 
extraction processes utilizing hydrocarbon solvents 
shall be located in a room or area dedicated to 
extraction.

❖ Equipment should be set up in a single location 
and not be relocated or moved as part of the 
process, as frequent movement of equipment 
could lead to failure in hoses or connections, 
causing a release of flammable solvents. The 
area should be dedicated to processing or 
extraction activities to reduce the risk of failure 
impacting a larger area or putting occupants at 
risk. 
3903.4 Post-process 
purification 
and 
winterization. Post-processing and winterization 
involving the heating or pressurizing of the miscella 
to other than normal pressure or temperature shall 
be approved and performed in an appliance listed 
for such use. Domestic or commercial cooking 
appliances shall not be used.

❖ The heating or pressurizing of the miscella can 
release remnants of the solvent as flammable 
vapors. Domestic and commercial cooking 
appliances are not listed for use in flammable 
vapor atmospheres. 
3903.4.1 
Industrial ovens. The use of 
industrial ovens shall comply with Chapter 30.

❖ Requirements for industrial ovens are covered 
in 
Chapter 
30. 
This 
section 
requires 
compliance with those provisions. Essentially, 
a pointer is provided as to where the 
appropriate requirements are located for such 
processing. 
3903.5 Use of flammable and combustible 
liquids. The use of flammable and combustible 
liquids for liquid extraction processes where the 
liquid is boiled, distilled or evaporated shall be 
located within a hazardous exhaust fume hood, 
rated for exhausting flammable vapors. Electrical 
equipment used within the hazardous exhaust fume 
hood shall be rated for use in flammable 
atmospheres. Heating of flammable or combustible 
liquids over an open flame is prohibited. 
Exception: The use of a heating element not rated 
for flammable atmospheres, where documentation 
from the manufacture, or approved testing 
laboratory indicates the element is rated for heating 
of flammable liquids.

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600_sec3902_1_3cd3cc52
chunks.code_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600
chunks.content:
❖ Flammable or combustible liquids heated up to 
or above their flash points have the potential to 
develop vapor-air mixtures above the lower 
flammable limit (LFL). The use of a listed fume 
hood 
prevents 
an 
accumulation 
of 
concentrations in the flammable range. The 
use of listed electrical equipment along with the 
-

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600_sec3904_0_646858d4
chunks.code_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600
chunks.content:
CHAPTER 39—Processing and Extraction Facilities

SBC 801-CC-2024 
1435

prohibition on the use of open flame addresses 
process-related sources of ignition.

The exception recognizes the use of nonlisted 
equipment where evaluated by an approved 
testing laboratory as safe for use in flammable 
vapor environments. 
3903.6 Liquefied 
petroleum 
gas. 
Liquefied 
petroleum gases shall not be released to the 
atmosphere except where released in accordance 
with Section 7.3 of NFPA 58.

❖ Venting of LP-gas to the atmosphere shall 
occur only where the requirements of NFPA 58, 
Section 7.3 are met. The requirements limit the 
risk potential of introducing flammable vapors 
to the interior of the building by: limiting the 
maximum flow; limiting the release to a finite 
amount between shutoff valves; using bleeder 
valves; limiting a release to spaces designed in 
accordance with Chapter 10 of NFPA 58 to 
address safety concerns; and purging, in 
accordance with Section 7.3.2, in emergency 
situations. These requirements of NFPA 58, 
Section 7.3 limit the amount of flammable 
vapor being released or limit such releases to 
spaces designed specifically for that purpose. 
SECTION 3904 SYSTEMS AND 
EQUIPMENT 
3904.1 General requirements. Systems and 
equipment used with the processing and extraction 
of oils and products from plants shall comply with 
Sections 3904.2 through 3904.2.2.3 and 5003.2, and 
other applicable provisions of this code, SBC 201 
and SBC 501.

❖ Devices used in processing or extraction are to 
meet the requirements of Sections 3904.2 
through 3904.2.2.3, Section 5003.2, SBC 201 
and SBC 501. The provisions found within 
Section 3904 recognize that many of the 
systems and equipment are not typically listed 
and, therefore, specific requirements are 
necessary to assist in the evaluation and 
approvals process for the Fire official. 
3904.2 Systems and equipment. Systems or 
equipment used for the extraction of oils from plant 
material shall comply with Section 3904.2.1 or 
3904.2.2.

❖ Systems or equipment for extraction shall be 
listed or approved for the use. Section 3904.2 
provides two paths for compliance. Section 
3904.2.1 addresses equipment or systems that 
are specifically listed for extraction of oils from 
plant material. Section 3904.2.2 recognizes 
that this industry is fairly new and there may be 
the need to use nonlisted equipment. This 
section provides requirements to properly 
approve 
nonlisted 
equipment. 
Section 
3904.2.2 and its subsections contain several 
additional requirements to provide the same 
robust design obtained through the listing. This 
includes prescribing the proper qualifications 
and approvals process, including a site 
inspection after installation. 
3904.2.1 
Listings. Systems or equipment 
used for the extraction of oils from plant material 
shall be listed and labeled in accordance with UL 
1389 and installed in accordance with the listing 
and the manufacturer’s installation instructions.

❖ UL 1389 was developed to investigate plant 
extraction equipment that utilizes flammable 
solvents. The use of listed equipment and 
systems provides an approval path that does 
not require a technical report and registered 
design professional site inspection since the 
listing takes into account many of the concerns 
for nonlisted equipment and systems. 
3904.2.2 
Approvals. Systems or equipment 
used for the extraction of oils from plant material 
shall be approved for the specific use. The system 
shall be reviewed by a registered design 
professional. The registered design professional 
shall review and consider any information provided 
by the system’s designer or manufacturer. A 
technical report in accordance with Section 
3904.2.2.1 shall be prepared and submitted to the 
Fire official for review and approval. The firm or 
individual preparing the technical report shall be 
approved by the Fire official prior to performing the 
analysis.

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600_sec3904_1_98bf3460
chunks.code_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600
chunks.content:
❖ Where extraction systems and equipment do 
not have a listing in accordance with Section 
3904.2.1, such systems and equipment shall 
be 
reviewed 
by 
a 
registered 
design 
professional, typically an engineer. A technical 
report analyzing the equipment is to be 
submitted to the Fire official for review and 
approval. The registered design professional 
shall demonstrate knowledge of the field and 
equipment by submitting qualifications to the 
Fire official for approval prior to submitting the 
technical report. Essentially, it allows the Fire 
official to sign off on the technical capability of 
the reviewer. 
3904.2.2.1 
Technical report. A technical 
report, reviewed and approved by the Fire official 
as required by Section 3904.2, is required prior to 
the equipment being located or installed at the 
facility. The report shall be prepared by a registered 
design professional or other professional approved 
by the Fire official. 
-

CHAPTER 39—Processing and Extraction Facilities

SBC 801-CC-2024 
1436

❖ This section is simply ensuring that a technical 
report is prepared and that such preparation is 
by a registered design professional. This report 
is required to be submitted to the Fire official 
before 
equipment 
can 
be 
installed, 
in 
accordance with Section 3904.2.2. This 
provides a mechanism to review the safety of 
the installation by a qualified third party. 
3904.2.2.2 
Report content. The technical 
report shall contain all of the following: 
1. Manufacturer information. 
2. Preparer of record of the technical report. 
3. Date of review and report revision history. 
4. Signature page, including all of the 
following: 
4.1. Author of the report. 
4.2. Date of report. 
4.3. Date and signature of registered 
design professional of record performing 
the design or peer review. 
5. Model number of the item evaluated. If the 
equipment is provided with a serial number, 
the serial number shall be included for 
verification at the time of site inspection. 
6. Methodology of the design or peer review 
process used to determine minimum safety 
requirements. Methodology shall consider 
the basis of design, and shall include a code 
analysis and code path to demonstrate 
whether specific codes or standards are 
applicable. 
7. Equipment description. A list of every 
component and subassembly, such as 
fittings, hose, quick disconnects, gauges, site 
glass, gaskets, valves, pumps, vessels, 
containers and switches, of the system or 
equipment, indicating the manufacturer, 
model 
number, 
material 
and 
solvent 
compatibility. Manufacturer’s data sheets 
shall be provided. 
8. A general flow schematic or general process 
flow diagram of the process. Post-processing 
or winterization shall be included in this 
diagram. Primary components of the process 
equipment shall be identified and match the 
equipment list required in Item 7. Operating 
temperatures, pressures and solvent state of 
matter shall be identified in each primary step 
or component. A piping and instrumentation 
diagram (PID or P&ID) shall be provided. 
9. 9.Analysis of the vessel(s) if pressurized 
beyond standard 
atmospheric pressure. 
Analysis shall include purchased and 
fabricated components. 
10. Structural analysis for the frame system 
supporting the equipment. 
11. Process safety analysis of the extraction 
system, from the introduction of raw product 
to the end of the extraction process. 
12. Comprehensive process hazard analysis 
considering failure modes and points of 
failure throughout the process. The process 
hazard analysis shall include a review of 
emergency procedure information provided 
by the manufacturer of the equipment or 
process and not that of the facility, building 
or room. 
13. Review 
of the assembly 
instructions, 
operational 
and 
maintenance 
manuals 
provided by the manufacturer. 
14. List of references used in the analysis.

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600_sec3904_2_5b5a6de1
chunks.code_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600
chunks.content:
❖ This section specifies the information to be 
presented 
in 
the technical report. 
The 
information to be provided includes many 
pieces of information related to the equipment. 
Since the equipment is not listed to a specific 
standard, the technical report needs to be 
robust in its requirements to properly evaluate 
the safety of the system and equipment. The 
report 
addresses 
items 
such 
as 
the 
manufacturer; the preparer of the technical 
report; date the report was prepared; signature 
and 
information 
on 
registered 
design 
professional; model number of the unit 
evaluated; 
methodology 
used 
for 
the 
evaluation; a description of equipment; a 
process 
flow 
schematic; 
analysis 
of 
pressurized vessels; structural analysis of 
equipment support frames; a complete process 
analysis; a hazard analysis that covers failure 
modes and points of failure; a review of 
assembly instructions and a list of the 
references used in the preparation of the 
report. This is necessary documentation for 
often unique designs. This also provides a 
consistent approach for any similar operations 
within a jurisdiction. 
3904.2.2.3 
Site inspection. Prior to operation 
of the extraction equipment, where required by the 
Fire official, the engineer of record or approved 
professional, as approved in Section 3904.2, shall 
inspect the site of the extraction process once 
equipment has been installed for compliance with 
the technical report and the building analysis. The 
-

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600_sec3905_0_f12c8bb8
chunks.code_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600
chunks.content:
CHAPTER 39—Processing and Extraction Facilities

SBC 801-CC-2024 
1437

engineer of record or approved professional shall 
provide a report of findings and observations of the 
site inspection to the Fire official prior to the 
approval of the extraction process. The field 
inspection report authored by the engineer of record 
shall include the serial number of the equipment 
used in the process and shall confirm that the 
equipment installed is the same model and type of 
equipment identified in the technical report.

❖ The Fire official can require that the engineer 
of record or approved professional conduct a 
site visit to verify installation of the equipment. 
A report verifying that the equipment was 
installed per the technical report shall be 
submitted. This is necessary to ensure that the 
systems 
and 
associated 
equipment 
are 
constructed and in use as approved, which 
provides a level of accountability. 
SECTION 3905 SAFETY SYSTEMS 
3905.1 Gas detection. For extraction processes 
utilizing flammable gases as solvents, a gas 
detection system complying with Section 916 shall 
be provided.

❖ Where a flammable gas is used as a solvent, 
a continuous gas detection system shall be 
provided in accordance with Section 916. To 
prevent any accidental or planned release of 
flammable gases from reaching the lower 
explosive 
limit/lower 
flammability 
limit 
(LEL/LFL),the detection shall be set at 25 
percent of the LEL/LFL. This criterion is found 
in Section 916.8. Sections 3905.1.1 through 
3905.2 address the specific requirements 
associated with processing and extraction. 
3905.1.1 
Operation. Activation of the gas 
detection system shall result in all the following: 
1. Initiation of distinct audible and visual alarm 
signals in the extraction room. 
2. Deactivation of all heating systems located in 
the extraction room. 
3. Activation of the mechanical ventilation 
system, where the system is interlocked with 
gas detection. 
4. De-energize all light switches and electrical 
outlets.

❖ The required local alarm is intended to alert the 
occupants to an emerging hazardous condition 
in the vicinity. As a safety precaution, heating 
systems are required to be deactivated and 
light switches and electrical outlets to be 
deenergized. 
Both are 
potential ignition 
sources when flammable vapors are present. 
In addition to causing alarms to activate, the 
detection system must also activate the 
mechanical ventilation system. 
3905.1.2 
Failure of the gas detection 
system. Failure of the gas detection system shall 
result in the deactivation of the heating system; 
activation of the mechanical ventilation system 
where the system is interlocked with the gas 
detection system; and initiation of a trouble signal 
to sound in an approved location.

❖ as detection systems must be designed to be 
selfmonitoring and fail safe in that if the gas 
detection system is not operational the heat 
must be shut down and the ventilation system 
must operate. An alarm to the occupants is not 
necessary, but a trouble signal must be 
provided to ensure that the situation is rectified. 
3905.2 Emergency shutoff. Extraction processes 
utilizing gaseous hydrocarbon-based solvents shall 
be provided with emergency shutoff systems in 
accordance with Section 5803.1.3.

❖ In order to maintain control over the flow of 
flammable gases under emergency conditions, 
each supply source, as well as each point of 
use of flammable gas, must have an approved 
emergency shutoff valve. These valves must 
be excess flow control valves to regulate the 
rate of flow of hazardous materials within the 
piping system, fail-safe valves or other 
approved types that will operate automatically. 
There must also be a manual means of 
operation to stop the flow.

-

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600_sec5001_0_a630087f
chunks.code_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600
chunks.content:
CHAPTER 50—HAZARDOUS MATERIALS—GENERAL PROVISIONS 
 
  
SBC 801-CC-2024 
1443 
 
—HAZARDOUS MATERIALS—GENERAL 
PROVISIONS

❖ General Comments 
 
The requirements of this chapter apply to all 
hazardous chemicals. Hazardous chemicals 
are 
defined 
as 
those 
that 
pose 
an 
unreasonable risk to the health and safety of 
operating or emergency personnel, the public 
and the environment if not properly controlled 
during 
handling, 
storage, 
manufacture, 
processing, packaging, use, disposal or 
transportation. The requirements of this 
chapter 
and 
associated 
chapters 
are 
considered the minimum safety requirements 
for the use, production and storage of 
hazardous chemicals.

❖ Purpose 
 
This chapter contains the general requirements 
for hazardous chemicals in all occupancies. 
The general provisions of this chapter are 
intended to be companion provisions with the 
specific requirements of Chapters 51 and 53 
through 67 regarding a given hazardous 
material. 
SECTION 5001 GENERAL 
5001.1 Scope. Prevention, control and mitigation 
of dangerous conditions related to storage, 
dispensing, use and handling of hazardous materials 
shall be in accordance with this chapter. 
This chapter shall apply to all hazardous materials, 
including those materials regulated elsewhere in 
this code, except that where specific requirements 
are provided in other chapters, those specific 
requirements shall apply in accordance with the 
applicable chapter. Where a material has multiple 
hazards, all hazards shall be addressed. 
Exceptions: 
1. In retail or wholesale sales occupancies, the 
quantities 
of 
medicines, 
foodstuff 
or 
consumer products and cosmetics containing 
not more than 50 percent by volume of water-
miscible liquids and with the remainder of 
the solutions not being flammable shall not 
be limited, provided such materials are 
packaged in individual containers not 
exceeding 5 Liters. 
2. Application and release of pesticide and 
agricultural products and materials intended 
for use in weed abatement, erosion control, 
soil amendment or similar applications where 
applied 
in 
accordance 
with 
the 
manufacturers’ 
instructions 
and 
label 
directions. 
3. The off-site transportation of hazardous 
materials where in accordance with Ministry 
of Transportation (MOT) regulations. 
4. Building materials not otherwise regulated 
by this code. 
5. Refrigeration systems (see Section 608). 
6. Stationary storage battery systems regulated 
by Section 1207. 
7. The display, storage, sale or use of fireworks 
and explosives in accordance with Chapter 
56. 
8. Corrosives 
utilized 
in 
personal 
and 
household products in the manufacturers’ 
original consumer packaging in Group M 
occupancies. 
9. The 
use 
of 
wall-mounted 
dispensers 
containing 
alcohol-based 
hand 
rubs 
classified as Class I or II liquids where in 
accordance with Section 5705.5. 
10. Specific provisions for flammable liquids in 
motor 
fuel-dispensing 
facilities, 
repair 
garages, airports and marinas in Chapter 23. 
11. Storage and use of fuel oil in tanks and 
containers 
connected 
to 
oil-burning 
equipment. Such storage and use shall be in 
accordance 
with 
Section 
605. 
For 
abandonment of fuel oil tanks, Chapter 57 
applies. 
12. Storage and display of aerosol products 
complying with Chapter 51. 
13. Storage and use of flammable or combustible 
liquids that do not have a fire point when 
tested in accordance with ASTM D92, not 
otherwise regulated by this code. 
-

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600_sec607_0_0997b96c
chunks.code_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600
chunks.content:
CHAPTER 50—HAZARDOUS MATERIALS—GENERAL PROVISIONS

SBC 801-CC-2024 
1444

14. Flammable or combustible liquids with a 
flash point greater than 35°C in a water-
miscible solution or dispersion with a water 
and inert (noncombustible) solids content of 
more than 80 percent by weight, which do not 
sustain combustion, not otherwise regulated 
by this code. 
15. Commercial cooking oil storage tank systems 
located within a building and designed and 
installed in accordance with Section 607 and 
NFPA 30.

❖ The purpose of Exception 1 is to address 
consumer products, foodstuffs and cosmetics 
that are not formulated with flammable 
constituents and are stored and displayed in 
Group M occupancies, provided the individual 
package is limited to a volume of 5 L or less. In 
the event of a leak or broken package, the 
amount of hazardous material lost would be 
small and present a smaller spill cleanup 
problem. This is limited to products containing 
no more than 50 percent by volume of water-
miscible liquids and with the remaining content 
not being flammable, based on the code’s 
definition 
of 
“Flammable 
liquid.” 
This 
percentage limitation further reduces the 
hazard of a material spill due to the difficult 
ignitability of such materials. This exception 
would not apply to any Level 2 or 3 aerosol as 
defined in Chapter 2, because these consumer 
products 
are 
formulated 
with 
either 
a 
flammable propellant or a flammable base 
product. It also would not apply to many paints, 
coatings, adhesives or lubricants formulated 
with 
flammable 
or 
combustible 
liquids. 
Consumer products, such as household bleach 
(which is typically a mixture of less than 12.5-
percent sodium hypochlorite by volume and 
water) or window cleaners (typically formulated 
with 3 to 6 percent by volume of ammonium 
hydroxide) would be exempted by this 
exception, as would a 4 L bottle containing a 
solution of 37-percent hydrochloric acid and 
water used for disinfecting swimming pool 
water or for cleaning concrete surfaces.

Exception 2 states that the application of 
pesticides and agricultural products is not 
considered a hazardous process if done in 
accordance 
with 
the 
manufacturer’s 
instructions and label directions, since those 
products and pesticides have been tested in 
these application methods.

Exception 3 states that any time hazardous 
materials are transported over public highways 
or by public transportation. The code intends 
that when a material, good or commodity is 
transported off site, it is within the domain of the 
MOT while the material is in transit. The code 
has no authority when the material is in transit, 
including while shipping papers are being 
prepared or are in effect. Note, however, that 
while the code exempts hazardous materials in 
transportation, it does not exempt the facility 
where these materials are packaged and 
prepared for shipment.

Once the material reaches the point where it 
has been delivered and the shipping papers 
have been received by the consignee (e.g., the 
party responsible for the material), the goods or 
commodity are no longer within the domain of 
MOT and the applicable regulatory requirements 
are those adopted fire and building codes of the 
jurisdiction 
whose 
enforcement 
and 
interpretation is the responsibility of the Fire 
official.

Exception 4 exempts commonly used building 
materials that may exhibit some hazardous 
properties, but do not fall into the realm of a 
hazardous materials as defined in the code, 
from being regulated as hazardous material. 
Without this exception, the storage of certain 
building materials could result in a Group H 
occupancy classification.

Exception 5 states that refrigeration systems 
are governed by Section 608, which has 
specific requirements that would be enforced 
over 
any 
general 
requirements 
of 
the 
hazardous material chapters of the code.

Exception 6 notes that stationary storage 
battery systems are governed by Section 1207, 
which has specific requirements that would be 
enforced over any general requirements of the 
hazardous material chapters of the code. Note 
that SBC 201 considers this an incidental 
storage area. See Table 509 of SBC 201 for 
incidental use area requirements.

Exception 7 refers to Chapter 56 for specific 
requirements 
concerning 
fireworks 
and 
explosives. Those specific requirements would 
be enforced over any general requirements of 
the hazardous material chapters of the code.

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600_sec607_1_ce9349d1
chunks.code_id: SBC 801 - The Saudi Fire Protection Code (3)-1401-1600
chunks.content:
Exception 8 states that corrosives used in 
personal and household products, such as 
certain cleaners or detergents, are not 
considered hazardous when maintained in 
their original packaging in Group M areas. Note 
that this is specific to Group M use areas only. 
However, this exception does not apply to 
small containers of flammable aerosols or 
liquids in retail display areas where they are 
regulated in Chapters 51 or 57, respectively. 
Without 
this 
exception, 
many 
common 
products in mercantile display areas could be 
classified as hazardous materials resulting in 
-

CHAPTER 50—HAZARDOUS MATERIALS—GENERAL PROVISIONS

SBC 801-CC-2024 
1445

grocery 
stores 
and 
other 
mercantile 
occupancies being inappropriately classified in 
occupancy classification Group H.

Exception 9 provides a necessary cross 
reference to provisions in Section 5705.5 to 
prevent alcohol-based hand rub dispensers
